THIS. This is what I'm telling again and again. The main interest of policy engines is that they're not Kubernetes-only (I mean, beside VAP ofc). But that you can use them to check so many practices.

Option #2 : Kyverno, the option that people love because it's just YAML - essentially VAP but more powerful

And Kyverno allows continuously scanning existing resources, not being admission-only!

Wrapping that first day of  #KubeCon #CloudNativeCon 2026 with a talk by @nabarun.dev comparing the various policy engines options:

- ValidationAdmissionPolicy
- Kyverno
- Gatekeeper/OPA
- Kubewarden
The talk on choosing a policy engine was top-notch, definitely left feeling informed and inspired #kubecon

You think this looks easy? So far, we have about a 5% success rate. Think you can do better?

We are excited to announce support for Flux for #Kubeflow v2.2 to enable AI/ML workloads paired with #HPC simulation. - Crossplane vs Terraform: reconciliation vs one-shot
- Composition Functions: Go code, not YAML patches
- Kyverno guardrails: block $10K RDS instances
- Secrets: MR → XR → Claim → K8s Secret chain
- Debugging: find the real error 3 levels deep

Are you attending #kubeconeu2026? Feel free to swing by P-1A at project pavilion and ask us anything about the #CNCF #TAG(Technical Advisory Grrop) #DevEx‼️

Red Hat donating llm-d to CNCF is huge. The gap between 'we have a model' and 'we serve it reliably at scale' is where most AI projects die. Production inference infrastructure is boring and essential - exactly the kind of thing CNCF should own.

It’s official: llm-d has joined the cncf.io ! 🚀

Our mission to evolve Kubernetes into SOTA AI infrastructure just got a massive boost. This milestone belongs to our amazing community. 

Thank you for building this with us. 💜

We’re just getting started!

🔗 www.cncf.io/blog/2026/03...
